6:15am

The Seven
6:45am
Worship
1st at-bat
Scripture and prayer before the world asks anything.
Bible plan — Proverbs / Psalm / New Testament. Set the tone before the inbox sets it for you.
7:15am
Workout
2nd at-bat
Zone 2 cardio or mobility per the plan.
Move the body before the mind has anything to say about it. Heart rate honest, not heroic.
8:00am
Wellness
3rd at-bat
AM skincare. Plan today's nutrition. Decompress before kickoff.
Caldera + Lab routine. 200g+ protein across four meals planned. Hydration mapped. A measured hour before the field opens at 9.
9:00am
Work
4th at-bat
Open with the Communication block, then onto the territory.
First 25 min: Communication Mastery (Vol. I). Then prospect, call, meet, close. Rhythm target: 25 calls / 25 F2F per week.

F2F

9:00pm
Wealth
6th at-bat
Reading: markets, business, the long game.
AUD/USD positioning where relevant. The plan to age 56 doesn't build itself between meetings.
9:30pm
Wisdom
7th at-bat
From the current reading list.
One idea worth carrying into tomorrow. Write it on the day's note.
Anchors
8:00pm
Tracy
Family before phone.
The most important hour you don't schedule on purpose.
10:00pm
Wind down
PM skincare. Shave. Screens off. Review the scorecard.
Caldera + Lab PM routine, shave clean. Score the day, set the alarm, write tomorrow's top three.

Vol. I  ·  A 56-Day Program Adam · June 7, 2026

The Communicator

An eight-week discipline for becoming the most trusted voice in the room — and the one most worth listening to.
The Charge Twenty-five minutes a day. Eight weeks. The goal isn't to speak more — it's to be the person whose words land, whose stories stick, and whose presence shifts the temperature of a room before a slide ever appears.
Days Complete
0 / 56
of the 8-week arc
Current Week
1
Executive Presence
Streak
0
consecutive days
Progress
0%
of total program
Week One

Executive Presence

How you land before you speak. The body, voice, and stillness that earn you the right to be heard.
01
The Three Pillars of Presence
The Charisma Myth · Olivia Fox Cabane
Reading

Olivia Fox Cabane spent years studying why some people walk into a room and the air changes. Her conclusion: charisma isn't a personality you're born with. It's a behavior that breaks down into three observable components — presence, power, and warmth.

Presence is the most rare. It's the feeling that the person in front of you is fully here — not thinking about their next meeting, not waiting to talk, not glancing at their phone. When you have presence, the other person feels like the only person in the world.

Power is the signal that you have the ability to affect the world — through resources, expertise, intelligence, status. People are drawn to capability.

Warmth is the signal that you will use that power on their behalf rather than against them. Without warmth, power is threatening. Without power, warmth is dismissed.

You probably already have power. You may have warmth. The leverage point for most executives is presence — because it's the one most people don't even know they're missing.

Exercise

The Presence Test (15 min).

  1. For your next two conversations today (a rep call, a customer call, a chat with your son), put your phone face-down and out of reach before they start.
  2. When the other person speaks, do not plan your response. Listen to the actual words. Notice when your attention drifts — and bring it back without judgment.
  3. After each conversation, write down one detail you wouldn't have caught in a normal "half-listening" state.

That's it. Presence is built one rep at a time, like a muscle.

Reflection
Which of the three — presence, power, warmth — is your strongest? Which is the gap that, if closed, would change everything?
Notes
02
Anchoring — Stance, Center, Stillness
Executive Presence research · Sylvia Ann Hewlett
Reading

Watch a senior executive who commands a room. They don't fidget. They don't rock on their feet. They don't fill silences with "um" or "so." They are anchored.

The body broadcasts authority before the mouth opens. Hewlett's research found that "gravitas" — the executive presence quality — is overwhelmingly read off physical cues: how you stand, how you occupy space, how you handle silence.

Three anchors:

Stance. Feet roughly shoulder-width, weight evenly distributed, knees soft. No weight-shifting. No hands in pockets when you want to project authority.

Center. Imagine a line from the crown of your head straight down through your spine into the floor. Shoulders back and down — not military, but open. Chest available, not collapsed.

Stillness. The hardest one. Most people move constantly — adjusting clothes, touching their face, shuffling notes. Stillness is what makes a person look like they belong wherever they are.

Exercise

The Mirror Drill (15 min).

  1. Stand in front of a full-length mirror. Set a 60-second timer. Just stand. Feet planted, hands relaxed at sides, eyes forward. Most people last 15 seconds before they fidget. Notice every urge to move.
  2. Now deliver this sentence to the mirror three times, staying anchored: "I'm Adam Greenberg, and I lead the Body Composition business at ImpediMed."
  3. Record one take on your phone. Watch it back with the sound off. Watch only the body. Is it still? Is it open? Is it credible?
Reflection
What does your body do when you're nervous that you'd never let it do on purpose?
Notes
03
The Power Pause
Talk Like TED · Carmine Gallo
Reading

Watch any speaker the audience trusts and you'll notice something counterintuitive: they slow down. They use silence the way a great chef uses salt — sparingly, deliberately, and exactly where it lands hardest.

Amateur speakers are afraid of silence. They believe silence is dead air, and dead air is failure. So they fill it with "uh," "right," "so basically," and rapid-fire delivery. They sound urgent — which, paradoxically, makes them sound small.

Senior leaders pause. They pause before a critical word. They pause after a key claim. They pause when asked a hard question — not to stall, but to demonstrate the question deserves consideration.

The power pause does three things: it lets the listener catch up, it signals confidence (only the secure can tolerate silence), and it makes whatever comes next feel more important.

The rule of thumb: take whatever pause feels natural and double it.

Exercise

The Pause Drill (15 min).

  1. Pick a 60-second statement you might give in a sales meeting — your standard SOZO opening, or your elevator pitch.
  2. Record yourself delivering it the way you normally would.
  3. Now rewrite the same script with three deliberate pauses marked: one before your most important word, one after your strongest claim, one before your final sentence.
  4. Re-record. Listen to both. The pauses will feel unbearably long to you. They will feel powerful to the listener.
Reflection
Where do you currently fill silence out of nerves rather than purpose?
Notes
04
Vocal Architecture — Pitch, Pace, Volume
Voice work · Cicely Berry / RSC tradition
Reading

Your voice is an instrument with three dials: pitch (high/low), pace (fast/slow), and volume (loud/soft). Most people use the same setting all the time. That's why most people are forgettable.

The voice that lands does the opposite of what's expected:

Drop pitch for authority. Going up at the end of a sentence (called "upspeak") makes a statement sound like a question. Drop the last syllable into your lower register and a statement becomes a verdict.

Slow down for the important parts. If everything is delivered at the same pace, nothing is emphasized. Slowing on a single word is a vocal underline.

Drop volume — don't raise it — for emphasis. Amateur speakers shout the punchline. Master speakers go quieter. The audience leans in.

Think of James Earl Jones, Oprah, Obama. They are not loud. They are resonant. They use the full range of all three dials.

Exercise

The Range Drill (15 min).

  1. Take this sentence: "SOZO is the only L-Dex–validated bioimpedance spectroscopy device cleared for lymphedema assessment in the United States."
  2. Read it five times. Each time, vary one element:
    • Drop the pitch on "only."
    • Slow down dramatically on "L-Dex–validated."
    • Drop your volume on "lymphedema assessment."
    • Combine all three on one take.
    • One more take — but this time pause for a full second before "only."
  3. Record the final version. Compare to your normal delivery.
Reflection
Which of the three dials — pitch, pace, volume — do you under-use the most?
Notes
05
Eye Contact That Lands
The Charisma Myth · Olivia Fox Cabane
Reading

Eye contact is the most powerful and most misused tool in communication. Too little, and you look evasive or unsure. Too much, and you look aggressive. The sweet spot is specific — and it can be trained.

The 3–5 second rule. In one-on-one conversation, hold eye contact for 3–5 seconds, then naturally shift (to a slide, a notebook, a different part of the listener's face) for a beat, then come back. Locking on too long reads as interrogation. Looking away too often reads as deception.

In a group, talk to one person at a time. Don't sweep your eyes across the audience like a lighthouse. Pick one person, deliver one full thought to them, then move to another person for the next thought. Each person feels personally addressed.

Eye contact is most powerful when you're listening, not when you're speaking. When the other person is talking, your eyes say "I am with you, and what you're saying matters." That is presence.

Exercise

The Listening Eyes Drill (15 min).

  1. In your next conversation today, count silently to three before you respond after they finish speaking. Hold their eyes during that pause.
  2. In your next group meeting (Zoom counts — look at the camera, not the gallery), deliver each separate thought to one specific person. Notice how it changes the energy.
  3. After both, journal one line: where did eye contact create connection? Where did it feel uncomfortable, and why?
Reflection
Whose eye contact, from someone you've met, has made you feel most "seen"? What were they actually doing?
Notes
06
The Confidence Reset
The Charisma Myth · Olivia Fox Cabane
Reading

Even after the body and voice are trained, internal state leaks out. If you walk into a CEO pitch feeling like an imposter, your body will betray you no matter how good your stance is. So presence is not just external — it requires an internal practice.

Cabane calls it the Confidence Reset. Before any high-stakes communication, you spend 60 seconds shifting your internal state from "performing" to "grounded."

The reset has three steps:

1. Acknowledge the discomfort. Don't pretend you're not nervous. Name it: "I'm a little wound up before this call." Naming reduces the grip.

2. Recall a recent win. A specific moment in the last 30 days when you were at your best — a deal you closed, a hard conversation you handled, a talk that landed. Feel that memory in the body, not just the head.

3. Set an intention, not a goal. Not "I need to close this." Instead: "I'm here to understand them and to be useful." Outcome anxiety drops the moment you focus on contribution, not extraction.

Exercise

Build Your Personal Reset (15 min).

  1. Write down three recent wins — specific moments, no older than 90 days, where you were at your best. Be precise: not "the Q1 numbers," but "the Atlanta call where the bariatric surgeon flipped from skeptic to advocate in 20 minutes."
  2. Pick the one that lights you up most. That's your reset memory.
  3. Write one intention you can apply before any high-stakes call: a sentence that starts with "I'm here to…"
  4. Test it before your next call this week. Even just 30 seconds in the car or before joining Zoom.
Reflection
What story are you telling yourself before you walk into a hard conversation? Is it the story that helps you, or the one that shrinks you?
Notes
07
Self-Audit Recording
Practice · Personal application
Reading

You can't fix what you can't see. Today is the baseline. The point of recording yourself at the end of Week 1 is not to be impressive — it's to know exactly where you're starting. In seven weeks, you'll record again. The contrast will be the proof.

Three reasons most professionals never watch themselves on video:

1. It's uncomfortable. Almost no one likes their voice or face on screen at first. Get past it.

2. They've never been taught what to look for. You will be, starting today.

3. The data is brutal. But it's the most honest coach you'll ever have. The camera lies less than your friends, your team, and your own memory of how it went.

This recording is for one viewer: future-you in seven weeks. Don't redo it. Don't polish it. Capture the raw baseline.

Exercise

Baseline Recording (20 min).

  1. Set your phone up in landscape mode at eye level. Stand to deliver — don't sit at a desk.
  2. Speak for 2–3 minutes, unscripted, on this prompt: "Why ImpediMed and SOZO? Why now? Why does this matter?" Imagine you're answering a curious CEO who has 3 minutes.
  3. Save the file. Label it "Communicator — Week 1 Baseline" and the date.
  4. Watch it back once. Make notes on three things only:
    • One body habit you didn't realize you had (fidget, sway, hand position).
    • One vocal habit (upspeak, filler word, monotone, rushing).
    • One moment where you actually sounded like yourself — confident and clear.
  5. Save the file somewhere you'll find it in Week 8.
Reflection
What surprised you? Not "what was bad" — what was different from how you thought you came across?
Notes
Be quick to listen, slow to speak, slow to become angry.
— James 1:19
Saved automatically to this device. Close the tab and come back tomorrow.
Focus

Presence, power, and warmth.

Olivia Fox Cabane spent years studying why some people walk into a room and the air changes. Her conclusion: charisma is not a personality you are born with. It is a behavior that breaks down into three observable components: presence, power, and warmth.

Presence is the most rare. It is the feeling that the person in front of you is fully here, not thinking about the next meeting, not waiting to talk, not glancing at a phone.

Power is the signal that you can affect the world through resources, expertise, intelligence, or status. People are drawn to capability.

Warmth is the signal that you will use that power on their behalf rather than against them. Without warmth, power feels threatening. Without power, warmth is dismissed.

The Presence Test · 15 min
  1. For your next two conversations today, put your phone face-down and out of reach before they start.
  2. When the other person speaks, do not plan your response. Listen to the actual words. When your attention drifts, bring it back without judgment.
  3. After each conversation, write down one detail you would not have caught in a normal half-listening state.

Presence is built one rep at a time, like a muscle.

JUDG · 2 The Decisions Discipline · the calibration log

The Decisions Journal.

Every important decision logged with reasoning, confidence, and a review date. Three months later you return and write what actually happened. Over five years this becomes a calibration tool that compounds judgement more than any executive program. What you reread is what you learn from.

COMPASS · 4 The Orientation · manifesto · reflection · bucket

The Compass.

Plays are the campaigns. The Compass is the heading. Three documents that orient the 25-year arc: who you are (manifesto), what you learned (annual reflection), where you want to go (bucket list). Reviewed quarterly, rewritten annually, never rushed.
The Lifetime ArcTodayNorth StarReturn

Twenty-Five Years.

Movement I
The Season · build
2026 → 2029 · ~3 years

Movement II
The Plateau · operate
2029 → 2041 · ~12 years
Operating at peak. Leading at scale. Wealth compounding in USD and AUD. Mentoring the next generation. Sons becoming fathers. Australian property anchor producing.
Movement III
The Return · wind home
2041 → 2051 · ~10 years
Winding down US operations. Transitioning back to Queensland. Family anchor activated. Grandchildren years. The long tail of impact.

Manifesto · who I am, what I stand for

never edited

Prompts to start

  • Who am I, at my best? Write it as a sentence a stranger would recognize.
  • What three things am I unwilling to compromise on — ever?
  • What does the version of me at the end of the 1,000 days look like? What about at the end of the 25-year arc?
  • How do I want my wife, my sons, the people I lead, the people I serve, to describe me?
  • What's the one belief about myself that — if I really lived it — would change everything?
  • What am I building, ultimately, and for whom?
No manifesto written yet. Tap Edit to begin.

Annual Reflection · memory of the year

Once a year, write down what actually happened. The wins, the losses, the lessons, the people, the version of you that emerged. Year five of these is when it becomes priceless — you can read back across the arc and see who you were.

Year
Date Written
Title — a phrase that names the year
Prompts What worked? · What didn't? · What did I learn? · Who did I become? · What surprised me? · Who showed up for me, and who did I show up for? · What's the single biggest lesson? · What am I taking into next year, and what am I leaving behind?
Reflection
No reflections written yet. At the end of each year, sit down and write one. Future you will read these.
